title: Executive Order 12139—Foreign Intelligence Electronic Surveillance

flags.justification: The Attorney General's authority is narrowly bounded by the underlying Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act's own certification requirements; for most surveillance, an independent FISA Court order is required. This order faithfully implements that statutory architecture rather than circumventing it.

Sec. 1-103 requires certifying officials to be Presidentially-appointed, Senate-confirmed officials specifically — a genuine, textually explicit safeguard against delegating certification authority to lower-level, unconfirmed officials.
Single coherent purpose: implementing FISA's authorization procedures.
This order implements a detailed statute (FISA) with its own extensive definitions and procedures; it designates who exercises delegated authority rather than creating new standards.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
Implements a permanent statutory framework; evergreen, standing national-security authorization structure.

Secs. 1-104 and 1-105 explicitly require surveillance to be conducted 'in accordance with that Act as well as this Order' — reinforcing rather than displacing the FISA Court's independent judicial oversight for the relevant categories of surveillance.

The independent, Article III FISA Court provides genuine external judicial oversight for court-order surveillance, and the Senate-confirmation requirement for certifying officials (Flag 2) provides an additional accountability layer.

This order implements, without expanding beyond, a new statutory framework (FISA) that itself represents a major accountability reform relative to the prior, wholly unchecked surveillance regime that preceded it; the order establishes new checks rather than creating a concerning expansive precedent.
Officials from State, Defense, CIA, and FBI are each designated within their own proper domains; no mandate is redirected.
No coverage population or exemption structure is created.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 0/16 = 0.0%. Implements the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act of 1978 — the direct legislative response to the Church Committee's revelations of unchecked surveillance abuses. One of the most historically significant national-security orders in the corpus, scoring 0% precisely because it establishes and faithfully preserves genuine judicial and Senate-confirmation checks, in direct contrast to several other national-security-domain orders in this corpus that score highly (EO 9066, EO 9102, the Export Administration Act zombie-emergency chain). A strong candidate flagship example of well-designed governance.
